metric_value: Business Objects 1996 revenue: $85.1 million; 40% growth from 1995 to 1996
notes: Annual revenue figure from company about section

metric_value: Business Objects has 475,000 licenses representing broad usage within 4,000 enterprises across more than 60 countries
notes: Installed base size as of study date

metric_value: Q3 1996 loss of $300,000 due to difficulty stabilizing BusinessObjects 4.0 architectural transition
Q4 1996 profits of $1.8 million from revenues of $25.8 million
Business Objects has more than $40 million in cash reserves and zero debt
Business Objects workforce approximately 550 employees; dual headquarters in Paris France and San Jose California
Business Objects has approximately 450 value-added resellers (VARs)
Business Objects books approximately 70% of revenue from direct sale of software licenses; average size sale approximately $150,000
Telemarketing is responsible for sales between approximately $20,000 and $50,000
BusinessObjects 4.0 development required approximately 150 person-years and $15 million investment
BusinessObjects Microcube Engine builds compact multidimensional cubes on-the-fly obviating time-consuming pre-built cube construction; enables workgroup sharing via email
BusinessObjects schemaless approach to multidimensionality works with any relational schema; no need for star or snowflake schemas or proprietary MOLAP tools
BusinessObjects 4.0 Reporter (query and reporting): $595; Explorer (OLAP): $695; Designer module: $1,995; Supervisor module: $1,995; Document Agent Server: $4,995
BusinessMiner: $995 standalone or $495 when purchased with BusinessObjects 4.0
Aberdeen believes Project Darwin will significantly reduce maintenance and configuration management costs and drive down total cost of ownership; business users can reuse existing Universes
Project Darwin was released as WebIntelligence; became successful web-based BI product; concept validated as web delivery became standard for enterprise BI by 2000s
notes: Business Objects delivered web BI successfully; WebIntelligence was a key product in the SAP acquisition

metric_value: Aberdeen believes BusinessMiner will augment decision-support desktop and pressure high-end data mining software suppliers to offer more sophisticated tools at more competitive prices
Data mining and predictive analytics democratized through platforms like RapidMiner, Weka, and eventually Python/R; Aberdeen's directional prediction was accurate; BusinessMiner itself discontinued
notes: Democratization happened but via different tools than BusinessMiner

metric_value: Aberdeen believes Business Objects demonstrates well-established pattern of excellence; positions company as stable supplier choice in rapidly shifting decision-support marketplace
SAP acquired Business Objects January 2008 for $6.8 billion — largest acquisition in SAP's 35-year history; Business Objects had grown from $85M (1996) to $1.5B revenue (2007)
SAP designated BusinessObjects 4.0 as the sole decision-support tool provider in the SAP Complementary Software Partner Program — a highly privileged and exclusive designation
Business Objects holds privileged position in Microsoft Alliance for Data Warehousing
Business Objects had compound annual growth rate of 80% during the three years prior to study
Large user with 5,000-copy rollout plans concluded that combination of strong technology and maturity would translate into a sustainable platform for competitive advantage
notes: Key user quote from Aberdeen field interviews

metric_value: Business Objects executives report over 90% code reuse from BusinessObjects 4.0 in Project Darwin development
notes: Code reuse claim for Project Darwin

metric_value: Strategic resellers include Bull, Digital, Fujitsu, NEC, NCR, Price Waterhouse, Sequent, SHL, Siemens Nixdorf, Toshiba, and Unisys
Business Objects provides Rapid Deployment Templates (RDTs) for popular business applications including Oracle Financials, PeopleSoft and SAP
notes: Pre-built universe templates for ERP systems

metric_value: Enterprise users report helpdesk support as comparatively lackluster during 4.0 beta but improved; one user with 3,000 licenses claims helpdesk is weak link; another says Web interface improved support
notes: Mixed user feedback on helpdesk quality

metric_value: BusinessObjects 4.0 brings OLAP-style analysis (drill, slice-and-dice) to mainstream users without requiring proprietary OLAP databases or ROLAP star/snowflake schemas
notes: OLAP access for non-specialist business users

metric_value: Business Objects grew to $1.5 billion revenue by end of 2007; acquired by SAP for $7 billion (40% premium on market cap) in January 2008
